CalifOrnia
Fair POlitical
Practices Cornrnission
June 12, L99L
John L. Rafuse
Manager, covernment Relations
UnocaI corporation
Post office Box 7600
Los Angeles, cA 90051
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-91-2 3 2
Dear Mr. Rafuse:
You have requested advice on behalf of Unocal corporation
concerninq qifts, "informational naterials,rr and the new
provisionSl-of the Poriticar Reforn Act (the Actx) 2 liniting
gifts and honoraria. Because you have not identified any specific
public official 
on whose behaLf you are requesting advice, we
consider your request to be one for infornal assistance.5
OUESTTONS
1. 
Is an informational tour of a Unocal offshore drilling
platform, including transPortation to and fron the platforro and
iefreshnents, consj-dered to be a gift or an honorarium to the
public official?
2. 
If either the trave} or the food and beverage coutponent
of the informatj,onal tour is deemed.to be a gift, 
is such a gift
]inited by the new provisions of the Act?
1 Th" Ethics in covernment Act of 1990.
2 corr"r.r*"nt code Sections 810oo-91015. Al-1 statutory references
are to the covernment code unless otherwise indicated. 
corrnission
regulations appear at 2 california code of Regulations section
18ooo, et seq. A11 references to regulations are to Title 2, Di-
vj-sion 6 of the California code of Regulations.
3 fnformal- assistance does not provide the requestor with the
irnrnunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3), copy enclosed.)
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 958O4-O8O? . (916)322-5660


Our Fi■e No. I-9■-232
Page 2
CONCLUSIONS
1. 
An informatj-ona1 tour of a unocal offshore drilling
platform, including transportation to and from the platform and
refreshments, is not an honorarium to the public official. 
In the
context you have presented, we view them as gifts. 
However, in
some cases, the tour and the transportation component, where no
other means of reaching the tour site are available, may be
excluded from the definition 6g ttgiftt' and treated as intangible
informational material. This is because for some of the
officials, 
the tour is rr inforuationalrt; it is relevant to the
duties and functions of the recipient of the information. Since
the officials 
whon you have indicated an interest in inviting to
tour Unocal's offshore drilling platforn have different duties and
are affiliated 
with different public agencies, the value of the
tour and transportation to the tour site would not be a reportable
gift for sone of the public officials, 
but for others, it probably
wouLd be.
2. 
Yes. These gifts to elected local officials, 
elected
state officials, 
members of state boards or conmissions, and
designated employees of state agencies are subject to the Act's
statutory linits.
FACTS
Unocal Corporation is interested in launching a program
whereby it invites public officials to visit one of its caLifornia
offshore drilling 
pl,atforns as its g:uests. Unocal's intention in
undertakinq this progrram is to educate public officials 
about
offshore drilling 
platforns and safeguards, production methods,
and safety prog?ams. All state legislators and their staffs,
members of regrulatory boards and cooDissions, such as members of
the California Coastal Conmission, and 1oca1 (county and city)
off icial-s will be offered this special tour, which is not
available to the public generally, but which has been arranged
especially to provide information to these state and local pubJ.ic
officials.
The offshore dril1j.ng platforns are only accessible by a boat
or helicopter leased by Unocal. UnocaL has long-term contracts
wj,th companies which provide transportation to the offshore
drilLinq platform for i,ts eaployees and guests. UnocaL would 11ke
to provide the same transportati.on for the public officials 
to and
from the offshore drillinq platforD. 
There are tero offshore
drilling 
pl-atforns in caLifornia. 
one is located in northern
Ventura County and the other j.n Huntington Beach, California.
The tour is expected to take tlro hours. Unocal- also would
like to provide refreshments to the officials during their tour of
the platforn. 
This would consist of sandwiches and beverages.
Unocal estimates that the total cost to Unocal for the
transportation to the platform and refreshments is from $100 to
9300 per person.


Our Fi■e No. I-9■-232
Page 3
ANALYSIS
section 89502 (b) defines t'honorariumrr as any payment rnade in
consideration for any speech qiven, article published, or
attendance at any public or private conference, convention,
meeting, social event, mea1, or like gathering. Section 82044
defines itpaymentrr as a paynent, distribution, transfer, loan,
advance, deposit, gift or other rendering of money, property,
services or anything else of value, whether tanqible or
intangible .
Under this definition, pa)ment nust be given to the Public
officiaL in consideration for a speech or article or to secure the
official,'s 
attendance at an event. Payments, advances, and
reimbursements for travel and related lodqing and subsistence
generally are construed as qifts. 
(See, Section 89505 for state
officiats 
and section 89501 for elected loca1 officials. 
)
Applying this definition to your situation, it would appear
that tle inforroational tour of Unocal's offshore drilling
platform, including transportation to and from the platform and
iefreshments, is not an honorarium to the invited public
official-s. 
The next step in our analysis, therefore, is to
determine whether the informational tour, transportation to and
from the tour site, and refreshments served in connection
therewith, are trgiftsrr to the invited public officials 
under the
Act.
Section 82028 (a) defines ngiftt as any payment to the extent
that consideration of ecrual or qreater value is not received.
The term ttgiftrr does not include:
Informational roaterj.al such as books, reports,
panphlets, calendars, or periodicals' 
No pa)rment
for travel or reimbursement for any expenses sha1l
be deemed ttinfornationaL material. rr
Section 82028 (b) (1) .
Under this definition, anything of value given to a public
official 
without cost is a gift and must be reported unless
expressly exempted. (section a2o28.) Free infornational
material, such as reports or periodicals, are exPressly exempted
from the rrgift" definition.4
4 st.ff is in the process of drafting regrulations interpreting
honoraria and gifts, including the ttinfornational material'r
exception to the definition of gift. 
The commission will consider
these regulations in the near future. 
Thus, the advice contained
in this letter is subject to changTe.


Our Fi■e No. I-9■-232
Page 4
There is one caveat. Free informational naterials, which are
expressly excluded as gifts under the Act, nust be solelv
informational and infornational onl-v to the public officiaL or his
or her agency. In other words, the informational material must be
relevant to the duties and functions of the recipient of the
information. 
(See, fn re Nida (7977) 3 FPPC Ops. 1 (copy
enclosed), which provides that the purpose for the rr informational
materiallr exception fron the definition of gift is to insure that
the Act would not inhibit the flow of necessarv inforrnation from
private sources to government officials; 
Estate of Baneri ee (1978)
21 Ca1- 3d 527, 540, provides that exceptions to the general rule
must be narrolrly construed. )
In 1975, the Cotlnission interpreted ttinfornational Baterialrr
to incLude both tangibLe and intangible naterials. 
(In re
Spel-Iman (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 16 (copy enclosed.) In that case, the
Conmj.ssion considered whether a tour of a nuclear porder plant was
a reportable gift or whether it came within the exception for
rr inf ormat j.onal material.'r The tour of the nucLear porrer plant vras
not available to the public Aenerally, but was arranged especially
to provide information to state governDent through Mr. spellman, a
legi.slative official. 
The conmission was asked to assume that the
tour was arranged at lr{r. spe.L1man's request and that it would not
be provided for anyone other than a government official. 
The
Conmission concl-uded that, under the circumstances of that
particuJ-ar situation, the exception for "informational materj-aI'r
applied to the tour of the nuc]ear power p]-ant and the value of
the tour lras not a gift to the official.
There have been other instances, however, where lre have
viewed the "informational tourl to be a reportabLe gift to the
officiaL. 
(See, olson Advj-ce Letter, No. A-85-218, copy
enclosed.) According to the facts presented in your J.etter, it
appears that the informational tour of the offshore drilJ-ing
platform provided by unocaL is the kind of tour that we have
placed in the trinformationaL naterial-" exception. (Leidiqh Advice
Letter, No. A-89-248, copy enclosed.) The tour is of a privately-
owned facility 
to which public access j.s restricted. 
And, the
tour is beinq arranged specially to provide information to the
public official.
Travel or transportatj.on provided to the public officiaL in
connection with a tour generally does not fall- into this
exclusion. (Section 82028(b) (1).) 
The conmission has recogTnized
one exception, however, to the express language of the statute.
In those instances where the tour is considered to be
trinformational materialr' under the Act, we previously have advised
that travel expenses are part and parcel of the tour itself if the
travel- is necessary to the tour, and it is the only neans of


our\ rile No. r-91-2 3 2
Paqe 5
transportatj.on to the tour site.5 
(Duffv Advice Letter, No. A-84-
084; copy enclosed.) This appears to be the case here.
However, under the circumstances that you describe in your
letter, the tour and transportation to the tour site do not appear
to be linited to specific public officials. 
As noted aUove, in
order for the 'rinformational materialrr exception to app1y, the
informatj.on offered by Unocal, in this case the tour oi ttre
offshore drilling 
platform, Dust be relevant to the duties and
functions of the recipient of that infornation. 
Therefore, for
some of the public officials, 
the value of the tour and travel to
the tour site would not be a reportable gift, but for others, it
probably would be- In this regard, please note that the gift 
and
honoraria provisions of the Act place liroits on gifts received by
pubJ.ic officials. 
(Section 89501, $100o for loca1 elected
officials; 
Section 89504 and 89505, $250 for elected state
officials, 
members of state boards or coromissions, and designated
employees of state agencies. ) 6
The other costs associated with the tour are for
refreshments. The tour is approxi-nately tlro hours in length, and
you stated in your letter that Unocal would like to serve
sandwiches and beverages to the public officiaLs on the offshore
dril1j.ng platfom. 
The exclusion contained in Section 82028 (b) (1)
does not extend to the costs incurred for providing food and
beverage. (Upholt Advice Letter, No. A-82-147, copy enclosed.)
Such costs would be a reportable gift for all the invited
officials. 
(Duffv, supra. )
- Please note, in this regard, the transportation to whi.ch sre
refer as possibly coming within the rrinforhationaL materialrr
exception is onJ.y the actual means of transport to the offshore
drilling 
pl-atforn. In your situation, this includes the cost of
the helicopter or boat leased by Unocal,. The officiaL,s travel
expenses to the destination site itself are expressly excluded
fron the tt inf orurational naterial" exception. The destination
site, in this case, is the launch site of the helicopter or boat
to the p1atfontr.
" The exenption from the gift linits described in subdivision (a)
of Section 89506 does not apply in your situation. 
Neither of the
requisites are present. The travel is not j-n connection with a
speech given by a state public official 
(subdivision (a) (1) ), and
the traveL is not provided by one of the tlT)es of entities
designated in subdivision (a) (2). Section 89506(b) is the
relevant subdivision. Furthermore, the exclusion fron the gift
linits set forth in Section 89501 ior travel expenses and
reasonable subsistence does not appl,y to travel in connection with
gifts.


Our File No. I-9■
-232
Page 6
f trust this J.etter provides you with the guidance you trave
requested.
If you have any further guestions regarding this
matter, please do not hesitate to call Ioe at (916) 322-5901-.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting ceneral counsel
″
易
By: Deanne Stone
counsel, Legtal Division
SH: DS : aa
Enclosures
